raw_case:
Postendoscopic spine surgery bacterial meningitis requiring multiple surgical interventions: A complex case report
An uncommon but potentially life-threatening complication of spinal surgery is bacterial meningitis, To the best of our knowledge, all of the documented cases have developed following open procedures, likely due to the increased potential for cerebrospinal fluid exposure and dural injury associated with these approaches, and notably, none of these documented cases were due to an endoscopic surgical approach. The low prevalence of this condition explains the reasonable but unfortunate lack of information about its natural history and associated complications in the medical literature. We present a case of a patient who experienced bacterial meningitis following endoscopic surgical intervention for lumbar disc herniation. This complex case required multiple surgical procedures to achieve a complete resolution.

Introduction

Endoscopic surgery is a subtype of minimally invasive spinal surgery, and this field is characterized by its dynamic and continuous evolution to address the evolving needs of the healthcare community. Endoscopic surgery has several important advantages, including reduced postoperative pain, a reduced risk of muscle and bone damage, shorter hospital stays, accelerated rehabilitation, and a quicker return to work. Nevertheless, with the progression of endoscopic spine surgery and its expanding range of applications, the incidence of complications associated with this procedure is also on the rise. Endoscopic spinal surgery can result in a variety of complications, some of the most common of which are dural tears, perioperative hematoma, transient dysesthesia, nerve root injury, and recurrence. These complications can occur regardless of the surgical techniques used [1]. Despite the potential for these complications, the cumulative incidence of adverse events has been found to be notably lower in endoscopic spinal surgery when compared to traditional open surgical techniques [2].An uncommon but potentially life-threatening complication of spinal surgery is bacterial meningitis, in which the meninges are directly invaded by bacteria through a dural rupture accompanied by the leakage of cerebrospinal fluid [3]. It is crucial to differentiate between bacterial meningitis that develops after open spine surgery and that develops after endoscopic spine surgery. To the best of our knowledge, all of the documented cases have developed following open procedures, likely due to the increased potential for cerebrospinal fluid exposure and dural injury associated with these approaches, and notably, none of these documented cases were due to an endoscopic surgical approach [2]. The low prevalence of this condition explains the reasonable but unfortunate lack of information about its natural history and associated complications in the medical literature. We present a case of a patient who experienced bacterial meningitis following endoscopic surgical intervention for lumbar disc herniation.

Case report

A 43-year-old female presented with backache and radiculopathy radiated along the lateral surface of thigh and posterior calf reaching the dorsum of foot. This pain had progressed over the course of a few years until it became intractable, was not relieved by analgesia, rest, or lying down, and was nonresponsive to conventional conservative treatment. During examination, gait of the patient was normal. Additionally, the straight leg raise test was positive at 30 degrees, and there was normal muscle bulk. Notably, the patient had paresthesia across the L5 nerve root dermatome. Following the taking of a medical history and examination, a lumbosacral MRI was done and revealed a right-sided paracentral disc herniation at the level of L4-L5 (Fig. 1). Dynamic radiographs were also sent and did not show any signs of lumbar spinal instability. Blood investigations were done, and the patient was prepared for elective percutaneous lumbar endoscopic discectomy surgery.A right-sided inter-laminar endoscopic approach was chosen. Thus, the patient was intubated under general anesthesia, and a prone position was established. The L5-S1 level was confirmed using a C-arm imaging technique. A posterior 0.5 cm paracentral incision was done, followed by the insertion of a trocar and the subsequent introduction of an endoscopic camera.During the surgical procedure, the presence of thickened flavum was observed and subsequently excised on both the ipsilateral and contralateral sides. On the contralateral side, a 6 mm longitudinal dural tear occurred by accident. Discectomy and flavectomy were done, and the dural tear was left without primary closure to be healed conservatively.Following the surgical procedure, the patient received postoperative instructions to remain in a recumbent position for a duration of 72 hours. Additionally, the patient was administered acetazolamide and steroid injections. The patient was discharged home on the subsequent day.Day 5 postoperative, the patient complained of a clear fluid discharge from the surgical site. The medical team initially reassured the patient and started a treatment plan that included twice-daily dressing changes and supine bed rest.Day 7 postoperative, the patient sought medical attention at the outpatient clinic due to lightheadedness and ongoing clear fluid discharge from the surgical site. To address these symptoms, the patient was admitted to the hospital and underwent a second operation to securely close the wound using deep full layer sutures. Subsequently, the patient was discharged with the same instructions, including the application of dressing twice daily.Three days following the second intervention, the patient presented with significant low back pain accompanied by discharge of fluid from the surgical site, headache, and photophobia. The examination revealed that the patient's vital signs were as follows: a temperature of 38.3°C, a heart rate of 108 beats per minute, a blood pressure of 135/80 mmHg, and a respiratory rate of 21 breaths per minute. The Glasgow Coma Scale was normal. Neck stiffness was positive, while Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs were both negative. There was no evidence of a focal neurological deficit. As a result, she was readmitted to the hospital. Blood and serological investigations were conducted, and a neurological consultation was sought. Subsequently, another MRI scan was performed, which revealed ongoing leakage of CSF (Fig. 2). Consequently, it was decided to perform a reoperation using a tubular system to address the dural tear. Under general anesthesia, a lumbar puncture was performed to collect CSF for gram stain, culture, and sensitivity testing. Following this, a 3 cm paracentral incision was made, and tubular dilators were inserted. Bony drilling and exposure of the dura were carried out, and the dural tear was repaired using 6/0 vicryl sutures, with the application of dural regeneration matrix.Empirical administration of antibiotics was initiated while waiting for the culture and sensitivity test results. The subsequent analysis revealed sensitivity to vancomycin, prompting an immediate switch to this antibiotic regimen. The culture results confirmed the presence of Staphylococcus aureus. Additionally, a dosage of 50 mg of caffeine was administered to manage the patient's severe headache.On the fifth postoperative day, while still receiving in-patient care, she developed a scant surgical site fluid discharge and a diagnosis of surgical site infection was made, which was treated with ongoing antibiotics, twice-daily dressing changes, and close observation.On postoperative day 25, a notable improvement in the patient's condition became evident, marked by a gradual resolution of symptoms such as headache, photophobia, and improved appetite. Laboratory findings showed a decrease in inflammatory markers such as C-reactive protein (CRP), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and white blood cell (WBC) count, which supported the clinical improvements. Subsequently, the patient was discharged home for a 1-week period. Upon her return, she reported experiencing mechanical back pain that significantly disrupted her daily activities, primarily attributable to discitis. Following a comprehensive consultation with the patient, a transforaminal lumbar inter-body fusion (TLIF) procedure was decided (Fig. 3).After the TLIF procedure was performed, the patient underwent a 5-day period of postoperative inpatient care. After that, she was discharged with notably improved back pain scores and experienced a complete resolution of her radicular symptoms.

Discussion

while endoscopic spinal surgery offers promising benefits, the limited number of documented cases treated with this technique presents challenges in effectively documenting and studying side effects. Comparative studies between open and endoscopic surgeries in spinal procedures are notably limited in number. A study conducted by Page et al., focusing on discectomy operations, represents one of these rare comparisons. Despite a smaller sample size in the endoscopic surgery group compared to the open surgery group, it is worth noting that no infection-related complications were reported in the endoscopic group [2]. Another study, led by Chiu et al, conducted a comparative analysis, focusing on laminectomy for lumbar spinal stenosis. This study also reported an absence of infection-related complications in the endoscopic surgery group, aligning with the findings of Page et als. study on Discectomy operations [2,5]. Intraoperative incidental durotomy, characterized by CSF leakage noted during the operation or postoperative massive clear fluid drainage, is a well-documented but not uncommon complication in spinal surgery, with reported incidences ranging from 0.3% to 13%. Importantly, this complication is associated with an increased risk of postoperative meningitis, highlighting the clinical significance of early recognition and management of durotomies to prevent potential postoperative meningitis [4].The clinical course of this case involved the development of bacterial meningitis following endoscopic spine surgery. The patient initially presented with symptoms such as clear fluid discharge from the surgical site, lightheadedness, headache, and photophobia. These symptoms raised suspicion of a potential CSF leak and meningitis. Diagnostic investigations played a vital role in confirming the presence of meningitis and determining the causative pathogen. These investigations included blood and serological tests, as well as neuroimaging (MRI). Additionally, a lumbar puncture was performed to collect CSF for gram stain, culture, and sensitivity testing. Empirical administration of antibiotics was initiated while awaiting the culture and sensitivity test results. Once the pathogen was identified and found to be sensitive to vancomycin, prompt initiation of this specific antibiotic regimen was crucial. The choice of antibiotics should be guided by the causative microorganism and its susceptibility to specific antimicrobial agents [6]. In the management of bacterial meningitis, addressing the source of CSF leakage is important to prevent recurrent infection. This surgical intervention aimed to definitively close the source of CSF leakage, reducing the risk of recurrent infection [7]. Over time, the patient showed notable clinical improvement, marked by the resolution of symptoms such as headache, photophobia, and improved appetite. Laboratory findings, including a decrease in inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, and WBC count), supported these clinical improvements. Subsequently, the patient was discharged home for further recovery.In summary, the management of meningitis following endoscopic spine surgery involves a multifaceted approach that encompasses early recognition, diagnostic workup, targeted antibiotic therapy, surgical intervention to address the source of CSF leakage, and ongoing monitoring. This case highlights the complexity and importance of managing such complications in a timely and comprehensive manner to achieve a positive patient outcome [3].

Conclusion

In conclusion, our case report sheds light on an unusual occurrence of bacterial meningitis following endoscopic spinal surgery, a complication that has been predominantly associated with open procedures. As endoscopic surgeries gain popularity for their minimally invasive nature and potential for fewer side effects, it becomes imperative for the medical community to recognize and understand the full spectrum of associated complications. The limited number of documented cases related to endoscopic approaches highlights the need for increased awareness and further research to understand the risk factors, clinical course, and management strategies for postendoscopic spine surgery bacterial meningitis.

Ethics approval

Our institution does not require ethical approval for reporting individual cases or case series.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for their anonymized information to be published in this article.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 43-year-old female
- **Chief Complaint:** Backache and radiculopathy radiating to the leg
- **History of Present Illness:** Progressive, intractable back pain and radicular symptoms not relieved by conservative treatments over several years. Positive straight leg raise test (30 degrees) and paresthesia along L5 dermatome noted.
- **Past Medical History:** None stated
- **Personal History:** None provided
- **Family History:** None stated
- **Physical Examination:** Normal gait, no muscle atrophy, positive neck stiffness during meningitis symptoms, and no focal neurological deficits.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Pre-surgery: MRI revealed right paracentral disc herniation (L4-L5 level); dynamic radiographs showed no lumbar instability.
  - Post-surgery: MRI showed persistent CSF leakage. Lumbar puncture revealed Staphylococcus aureus.
  - Laboratory findings: Elevated inflammatory markers such as CRP, ESR, and WBC count early, which normalized with treatment. 
  - Surgery findings: Accidental dural tear during endoscopic discectomy; confirmed persistent CSF leakage during reoperations.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Evaluation of Postoperative Clear Fluid Discharge:**
  - **CSF Leak:** Strongly suspected due to the dural tear during surgery and confirmed by postoperative CSF leakage visualized on MRI. Other causes (seroma, infection) ruled out based on the clinical course.

- **Postoperative Symptoms Including Headache, Photophobia, Fever, and Fluid Discharge:**
  - **Bacterial Meningitis:** Suspected due to hallmark meningitis symptoms combined with confirmed CSF leakage on imaging. Diagnostic lumbar puncture supported diagnosis (microbiological evidence of Staphylococcus aureus).
  - **Other Causes of Fever (e.g., Viral Meningitis, Sepsis):** Excluded based on lab results, absence of focal neurological deficits, and confirmed bacterial pathogen.

- **Persistent Back Pain Post-Treatment:**
  - **Discitis:** Diagnosed based on clinical history and resolution of meningitis-associated symptoms. Symptomatic mechanical back pain triggered consideration of discitis associated with postoperative infections.
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
- **Postoperative Bacterial Meningitis:** Resulting from a CSF leak caused by intraoperative dural tear during lumbar endoscopic discectomy. This was complicated by **surgical site infection** and **discitis** secondary to the initial intraoperative injury and subsequent interventions.
- Diagnosis was supported by the development of typical bacterial meningitis symptoms, imaging studies confirming CSF leakage, microbiological identification of Staphylococcus aureus, and clinical improvement following targeted management (antibiotics, surgical re-interventions, and eventual spinal fusion).
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Postoperative bacterial meningitis due to dural tear caused by endoscopic discectomy, complicating with surgical site infection and discitis.

results.content:
### Resoning:

Step 1: Understanding the Chief Complaint
The patient is a 43-year-old female presenting with backache and radiculopathy radiating to the leg. These symptoms suggest some form of nerve compression or irritation in the lumbar spine, likely due to a disc herniation or spinal stenosis.

Step 2: Analyzing the History of Present Illness
The back pain and radicular symptoms have been progressive and intractable, not relieved by conservative treatments over several years. This indicates that the condition has been chronic and resistant to non-invasive management, warranting further intervention.

Step 3: Interpreting Physical Examination Findings
The physical exam reveals a normal gait, no muscle atrophy, and positive neck stiffness during meningitis symptoms. However, since meningitis is not part of the chief complaint or history, this finding might be irrelevant or possibly indicate a separate issue. There are no focal neurological deficits noted, which suggests that the primary issue may not involve significant nerve damage or compression beyond what's causing the radicular symptoms.

Step 4: Reviewing Ancillary Tests
Pre-surgery MRI shows a right paracentral disc herniation at the L4-L5 level, which aligns with the patient's symptoms radiating along the L5 dermatome. Dynamic radiographs do not show lumbar instability, which is important for planning the surgical approach.

Step 5: Considering the Surgical Intervention
The patient underwent endoscopic discectomy, during which there was an accidental dural tear. Post-surgery MRI shows persistent CSF (cerebrospinal fluid) leakage, and lumbar puncture revealed Staphylococcus aureus, indicating an infection.

Step 6: Evaluating Laboratory Findings
Elevated inflammatory markers such as CRP, ESR, and WBC count early on, which normalized with treatment, suggest that there was an inflammatory process, likely due to infection, which has been addressed.

Step 7: Synthesizing the Information
The patient had a disc herniation leading to radiculopathy, treated with endoscopic discectomy, during which a dural tear occurred, leading to CSF leakage and subsequently a Staphylococcus aureus infection. The infection was treated, and inflammatory markers have normalized.

Step 8: Considering Differential Diagnoses
Given the history and findings, the primary diagnosis is a postoperative complication following endoscopic discectomy, specifically a dural tear with CSF leakage and bacterial meningitis due to Staphylococcus aureus.

Step 9: Confirming the Diagnosis
The diagnosis is supported by the MRI findings of disc herniation, the surgical report of dural tear, postoperative CSF leakage, and the isolation of Staphylococcus aureus from lumbar puncture. The improvement in inflammatory markers with treatment further confirms the diagnosis.

### Answer:

Postoperative complications following endoscopic discectomy: dural tear with CSF leakage and Staphylococcus aureus meningitis ########.

<Step 2> Postoperative symptoms including headache, photophobia, fever, and fluid discharge led to suspicion of bacterial meningitis, supported by hallmark meningitis symptoms and confirmed CSF leakage on imaging.
<Step 3> Diagnostic lumbar puncture confirmed bacterial meningitis with microbiological evidence of Staphylococcus aureus.

<Step 4> Other potential causes of fever, such as viral meningitis and sepsis, were excluded based on lab results, absence of focal neurological deficits, and identification of the bacterial pathogen.
<Step 5> Persistent back pain post-treatment triggered consideration of discitis, which was diagnosed based on clinical history and resolution of meningitis-associated symptoms.
<Step 6> Postoperative bacterial meningitis was determined to result from a CSF leak caused by an intraoperative dural tear during lumbar endoscopic discectomy.
<Step 7> The bacterial meningitis was complicated by surgical site infection and discitis secondary to the initial intraoperative injury and subsequent interventions.

<Step 8> Diagnosis was supported by the development of typical bacterial meningitis symptoms, imaging studies confirming CSF leakage, microbiological identification of Staphylococcus aureus, and clinical improvement following targeted management.
<Step 9> Targeted management included antibiotics, surgical re-interventions, and eventual spinal fusion.
